title: Assessing recall bias in post-conflict food security and resilience study in south Wollo zone, Ethiopia
author: Muhyie, Ayal, et al.
materials: Recalls it more or less clearly
Furthermore, several statistical techniques are available to detect CMB in Stata, including Harman‚Äôs Single-Factor Test, the Marker Variable Technique, and the Correlation Matrix Approach.

As the first factor accounts for <50 % of the variance, it indicates that Common Method Bias (CMB) is not a problem for the data. The Marker Variable Technique also determined that CMB is not a problem for the retrospective food security and resilience analysis. The researchers selected a factor variable called woreda (which is district). The p-value for woreda was 0.130 which is greater than 0.05 confirming it is not significant meaning CMB is not a problem for the retrospective data. Furthermore, the multicollinearity test for the independent variables indicated that there are no variables that are highly correlated. In the statistical test, multicollinearity which could be a result of CMB was not detected. The maximum correlation between independent variable was 0.48 which is not high to determine presence of CMB. The Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) was also <3 for all independent variables indicating no serious multicollinearity affirming that CMB is not a problem for the retrospective study in our study context.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: The flashbulb memory test was used to analyse recall bias in food security and resilience studies. Moreno-Serra et al. [8] argued that ex-post health surveys that collect recall data from conflict-affected populations are reliable for empirical inference using standard regression methods with the Flashbulb test, which was developed in the fields of psychology and psychometry. In this research, the same method as that used by Moreno-Serra et al. [8] was used for food security and resilience studies. The empirical specification of conflict as adapted to reflect the food security outcome, is as follows.Yi=Œ±0+Œ±1Di+Œºiwhere Yi is the food security outcome for household i, Œ±0is the intercept that measures the impact of conflict on the outcome variable, Di is the conflict exposure of household i which takes the value of 1 if exposed or 0 if the household is not exposed to conflict. The general understanding is that in communities affected by conflict, all households are considered to be exposed to conflict impacts and Œºiis the sum of all errors.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: When retrospective data are used in a survey, there could be recall errors, which might be due to memory issues and the negative impact of conflict on people‚Äôs ability to recall past events [8]. This recall error affects the estimation of Œ±1.The interaction is indicated mathematically by the formula below:Œºi=œâi+Œµiwhere œâiis the survey recall error, and Œµi takes all other errors (observed and unobserved) that are considered random. Conflict exposure is correlated with household characteristics, which results in a more realistic model of recall bias.Yi=Œ±0+Œ±1Di+Xi‚Ä≤ax+œâi+Œµi

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: In this model with covariates, the condition required for recall errors not to bias Œ±1 is that the conditional independence assumption cov (Di|Xi, œâi) = 0 holds in the data. This means that recall errors are randomly distributed across conflict and non-conflict areas once observable characteristics are controlled for.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: Brown and Kulik [18] suggested a complementary model to understand recall bias, called the flashbulb test, which suggests that major events such as natural tragedies or traumatic events can be recalled through the use of well-known events and objectively corroborated. X is the presence of recall bias computed using pre- and post-conflict scenarios, as a comparison between two times is necessary.œâi={0ifZt‚àí1=Z^t‚àí1,byindividualiatthetimeofthepost‚àíconflictsurveyt1ifZt‚àí1‚âÝZ^t‚àí1,byindividualiatthetimeofthepost‚àíconflictsurveytwhere Zt‚àí1 is a well-known past event occurred at time ‚Äút ‚àí 1‚Äù (pre-conflict) and Z^t‚àí1, it denotes the recall of the event by individual i at the time of the post-conflict survey ‚Äút‚Äù. We can then test the conditional independence assumption in a model with covariates cov (Di|Xi, œâi) = 0 by estimating the following regression:œâi=Œ≤0+Œ≤1Di+Xi‚Ä≤Œ≤x+œÜiwhere œâi ‚àà {0, 1} is an indicator that takes the value of zero if individual i accurately retrieves the past event (recall accuracy by comparing responses of respondents to the actual timing of the event), and 1 if the individual retrieves the event inaccurately, Œ≤0 is the intercept, Di ‚àà {0, 1} is an indicator that takes the value of 1 for conflict-affected and 0 otherwise, Xi contains the control variables, including age of the household head, education in number of years of the household head, gender of the household head, and œÜiis the error term.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: For the flashbulb memory test, this research considers the official announcement of the death of the late Prime Minister of Ethiopia, Mr. Meles Zenawi. Respondents were asked to check if they recalled the event, and statistical inference was made as a proxy to determine the presence of recall bias in the food security and resilience study. A questionnaire was designed to assess the clarity of the recall, importance of the event, accuracy of the recall against known timelines, level of confidence of respondents on their recollection of the event, and their recall.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: The questionnaire was an integral part of the food security and resilience survey and was administered along with the main research undertaking. Stata was used as the analysis software while conducting the analysis using appropriate statistical approaches.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: Harman‚Äôs Single-Factor Test: One of the most commonly used techniques, Harman‚Äôs Single-Factor Test, assesses whether a single factor accounts for most of the variance in the dataset. If one factor explains >50 % of the total variance, CMB may be present [14]. The mathematical formulation of this test is based on exploratory factor analysis (EFA), where the total variance explained by the first factor is computed as:Œª1/‚àëŒªiwhere:Œª1 is the eigenvalue of the first unrotated factor,‚àëŒªi is the sum of all eigenvalues.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: Interpretation of Harman‚Äôs Single-Factor Test: If the first factor accounts for >50 % of the variance, it indicates potential CMB and suggests that additional remedies may be required.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: Marker Variable Technique: This technique introduces a theoretically unrelated variable, known as a marker variable, to detect potential method bias. If the inclusion of the marker variable significantly alters regression coefficients, CMB is likely influencing the results [19]. The statistical formulation of this test is:Y=Œ≤0+Œ≤1X+Œ≤2MV+Œµwhere:Y is the dependent variable,X represents the independent variables,MV is the marker variable,Œ≤2 captures the effect of the marker variable,Œµ is the error term

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: Interpretation of the Marker Variable Technique: A significant change in the coefficients after including the marker variable suggests the presence of CMB.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: Correlation Matrix Approach (Full Collinearity Test): High correlations among independent variables, particularly above 0.90, indicate the possibility of CMB. The mathematical formulation for detecting multicollinearity using a correlation matrix is:œÅij=Cov(Xi,XJ)œÉxiœÉxjwhere:œÅij is the Pearson correlation coefficient between variables and XiandXJ,Cov(Xi,XJ)is the covariance between the two variables,œÉxiandœÉxj and are the standard deviations of the respective variables.

steps.title: Techniques of data analysis
steps.instruction: Interpretation of Correlation Matrix Approach (Full Collinearity Test): If correlations exceed 0.90, it suggests multicollinearity that may be driven by common method variance.

steps.title: Testing common method bias
steps.instruction: The Common Method Bias test was conducted by deploying a combination different statistical methods: Harman‚Äôs Single-Factor Test, Marker Variable Technique and Correlation Matrix Approach to strengthen statistical validity of research findings for inference (Fig. 2).
